action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: stone, lake, hunter, spear, river, tree, bird, king, ship, farmer, boat, dog, hill, road
Adjectives: old, cold, red, big, blue, small, tall, dark
Verbs (write them in this base form): hear, feed, see, hold, find, carry, chase, watch
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a stone hear a lake
    six stones hear a lake
    a old stone hear a lake

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the dark tree find three big roads  =>  titi gereve vu re tika ruro rarapo
    the three spears watch the blue tree  =>  re vapi ruro re gigoreve vu dipo
    three red dogs hear the blue lake  =>  re vapi veda tapo didaguve vu kevi
    two blue trees see the three hunters  =>  re reteve vu vapi rurove du gike
    six blue boats carry the six hills  =>  re gidive kotu vapi tatigave kotu geda
    six dogs feed the cold spear  =>  re dope gigore didaguve kotu teto
    three boats watch a spear  =>  gigore tatigave vu dipo
    the dark lake watch the five ships  =>  re rugive ve re tika veda dipo
    a road see two tall spears  =>  kipu gigoreve du gere gike
    the five blue lakes see the five old hunters  =>  re gigugo reteve ve re vapi vedave ve gike
    five cold lakes find the three birds  =>  re kogave vu dope vedave ve rarapo
    the five tall trees hear the four hunters  =>  re reteve ka re kipu rurove ve kevi
    the four dark lakes carry the three rivers  =>  re dageve vu re tika vedave ka geda
    the four small farmers hear six dogs  =>  didaguve kotu re taro diteruve ka kevi
    five tall kings find two cold roads  =>  dope gereve du kipu vapevive ve rarapo
    the five blue stones hold the five kings  =>  re vapevive ve re vapi totave ve rutoto
    four tall farmers chase the four lakes  =>  re vedave ka kipu diteruve ka ruka

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to figure out the basic grammar of this language. Let me start by testing some simple sentences to understand word order, how articles/numbers work, and what the basic vocabulary words are. I'll test singular vs plural, definite vs indefinite, and try to identify which words are nouns, verbs, and adjectives.

Let me start with some basic probes:

PROBES:
1. a stone see a lake
2. the stone see a lake
3. the stone see the lake
4. the lake see a stone
5. the stone hear a lake
action.label: response

probes: a stone see a lake

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone see a lake"  =>  veda tota gike
2. "the stone see a lake"  =>  veda re tota gike
3. "the stone see the lake"  =>  re veda re tota gike
4. "the lake see a stone"  =>  tota re veda gike
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations, I can see:
- "veda" = see (verb)
- "gike" = stone (noun)
- "tota" = lake (noun)
- "re" appears to be the definite article "the"
- Word order seems to be: verb + article + noun + article + noun (or verb + noun + article + noun)

Let me test more to understand: how adjectives work, how numbers work, and the verb "hear" vs "see".

PROBES:
1. a red stone see a lake
2. the red stone see a lake
3. a stone hear a lake
4. the red lake see a stone
action.label: response

probes: a red stone see a lake
the red stone see a lake
a stone hear a lake
the red lake see a stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a red stone see a lake"  =>  veda tapo tota gike
2. "the red stone see a lake"  =>  veda re tapo tota gike
3. "a stone hear a lake"  =>  veda tota kevi
4. "the red lake see a stone"  =>  tota re tapo veda gike
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations:
- "veda" = see (verb)
- "kevi" = hear (verb)
- "tapo" = red (adjective)
- "tota" = lake (noun)
- "gike" = stone (noun)
- "re" = the (definite article)
- Word order: verb + article + adjective + noun + article + noun (for "the red stone see a lake")
- For "a red stone see a lake": verb + adjective + noun + noun (no article for indefinite)

Let me test more to understand: how numbers work, how adjectives work with numbers, and test other verbs.

PROBES:
1. two red stones see a lake
2. the two red stones see a lake
3. a red stone hear a lake
4. the red stone hear a lake
action.label: response

probes: two red stones see a lake
the two red stones see a lake
a red stone hear a lake
the red stone hear a lake

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two red stones see a lake"  =>  veda tapo totave du gike
2. "the two red stones see a lake"  =>  veda re tapo totave du gike
3. "a red stone hear a lake"  =>  veda tapo tota kevi
4. "the red stone hear a lake"  =>  veda re tapo tota kevi
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations:
- "veda" = see (verb)
- "kevi" = hear (verb)
- "tapo" = red (adjective)
- "tota" = lake (noun, singular)
- "totave" = lakes (noun, plural)
- "gike" = stone (noun, singular)
- "re" = the (definite article)
- "du" = two (number)
- Word order: verb + article + adjective + noun(plural) + number + noun (for "the two red stones see a lake")

Let me test more to understand: how other adjectives work, how other verbs work, and test the verb "hear" with different objects.

PROBES:
1. a cold stone see a lake
2. the cold stone see a lake
3. a stone hear a cold lake
4. the cold lake hear a stone
action.label: response

probes: a cold stone see a lake
the cold stone see a lake
a stone hear a cold lake
the cold lake hear a stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a cold stone see a lake"  =>  veda dope tota gike
2. "the cold stone see a lake"  =>  veda re dope tota gike
3. "a stone hear a cold lake"  =>  dope veda tota kevi
4. "the cold lake hear a stone"  =>  tota re dope veda kevi
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations:
- "veda" = see (verb)
- "kevi" = hear (verb)
- "dope" = cold (adjective)
- "tota" = lake (noun, singular)
- "gike" = stone (noun, singular)
- "re" = the (definite article)
- Word order for "a cold stone see a lake": verb + adjective + noun + noun
- Word order for "a stone hear a cold lake": adjective + verb + noun + verb (adjective before verb when object is modified)
- Word order for "the cold lake hear a stone": noun + article + adjective + verb + verb (subject first, then verb, then object)

Let me test more to understand: how other verbs work, and test the verb "hear" with different word orders.

PROBES:
1. a cold stone hear a lake
2. the cold stone hear a lake
3. a stone hear the lake
4. the lake hear the stone
action.label: response

probes: a cold stone hear a lake
the cold stone hear a lake
a stone hear the lake
the lake hear the stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a cold stone hear a lake"  =>  veda dope tota kevi
2. "the cold stone hear a lake"  =>  veda re dope tota kevi
3. "a stone hear the lake"  =>  re veda tota kevi
4. "the lake hear the stone"  =>  re tota re veda kevi

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the three red ships hold the four tall stones
2. the three spears hear the hill
3. a old dog feed the five roads
4. four dark birds hold two tall ships
5. the three kings chase two farmers
6. five red ships chase a red river
7. the three ships carry the two hills
8. a tall hill watch the two kings
9. two dark stones watch a cold farmer
10. four rivers hear the six birds

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me analyze each sentence based on the patterns I've discovered:

1. "the three red ships hold the four tall stones" - verb "hold" = rutoto, "red" = tapo, "ships" = veda, "tall" = kipu, "stones" = gike, "three" = vu, "four" = du, "the" = re
2. "the three spears hear the hill" - verb "hear" = kevi, "spears" = vapi, "hill" = ruka, "three" = vu, "the" = re
3. "a old dog feed the five roads" - verb "feed" = teto, "old" = gike, "dog" = taro, "five" = kipu, "roads" = gere, "the" = re
4. "four dark birds hold two tall ships" - verb "hold" = rutoto, "dark" = tika, "birds" = rarapo, "two" = du, "tall" = kipu, "ships" = veda
5. "the three kings chase two farmers" - verb "chase" = ruka, "kings" = rutoto, "two" = du, "farmers" = taro, "the" = re, "three" = vu
6. "five red ships chase a red river" - verb "chase" = ruka, "five" = kipu, "red" = tapo, "ships" = veda, "river" = tika
7. "the three ships carry the two hills" - verb "carry" = geda, "three" = vu, "ships" = veda, "two" = du, "hills" = ruka, "the" = re
8. "a tall hill watch the two kings" - verb "watch" = dipo, "tall" = kipu, "hill" = ruka, "two" = du, "kings" = rutoto, "the" = re
9. "two dark stones watch a cold farmer" - verb "watch" = dipo, "two" = du, "dark" = tika, "stones" = gike, "cold" = dope, "farmer" = taro, "a" = (no article)
10. "four rivers hear the six birds" - verb "hear" = kevi, "four" = du, "rivers" = tika, "six" = kotu, "birds" = rarapo, "the" = re

ANSWERS:
1. re vu tapo veda rutoto re du kipu gike
2. re vu vapi kevi ruka
3. taro gike teto re kipu gere
4. du tika rarapo rutoto du kipu veda
5. re vu rutoto ruka du taro
6. kipu tapo veda ruka tapo tika
7. re vu veda geda re du ruka
8. kipu ruka dipo re du rutoto
9. du tika gike dipo dope taro
10. du tika kevi re kotu rarapo
